cards.front: What's the difference between a router interface and a switch interface, in terms of L3 addressing?
cards.back: Router interfaces are LAYER 3 — each has its own IP/mask and is in its own subnet. Switch access ports are L2 — no IP; the switch SVI (interface vlan X) is the IP-bearing interface. Trunks carry multiple VLANs but no per-VLAN IP on a pure L2 switch.

cards.front: Configure an IP address and bring up router interface Gi0/0.
cards.back:
```
interface gi0/0
 ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown
```
Default state is `shutdown` on routers (unlike switch access ports). Verify with `show ip int brief`.

cards.front: What does `bandwidth` configured on an interface affect?
cards.back: It does NOT change the actual link speed. It's a parameter used by routing protocols (OSPF cost calculation: cost = ref-bw / bandwidth) and `show interfaces` output. Misconfigured bandwidth = wrong OSPF metrics. For accuracy, set it to match the actual link speed (in kbps).

cards.front: Why are loopback interfaces useful on routers?
cards.back: Always-up logical interface — never goes down unless administratively shut. Used as a stable router-id for OSPF/BGP, as a management IP, and as a source IP for control-plane traffic. Configure: `interface loopback 0` then `ip address X X`.

cards.front: What is the boot process of a Cisco router (4 steps)?
cards.back: (1) POST (Power-On Self-Test, ROM). (2) Bootstrap loads (ROM). (3) Locate and load IOS image (flash, then TFTP fallback, then ROMmon). (4) Locate and load startup-config from NVRAM (or enter setup mode if missing). Configuration register `0x2102` controls this; `0x2142` skips startup-config (used for password recovery).

cards.front: Memorize: what is the default configuration register on a Cisco router and what does it do?
cards.back: 0x2102 — normal boot: load IOS from flash, load startup-config. Other notable values: 0x2142 (skip startup-config — password recovery), 0x2120 (boot to ROMmon — diagnostics). Set with `config-register 0xNNNN` from global config.

cards.front: What does `show running-config` show vs `show startup-config`?
cards.back: `show running-config` (or `show run`): live config in RAM, includes any unsaved changes. `show startup-config` (or `show start`): saved config in NVRAM, what loads at next reload. They differ if you've made changes without `copy run start`.

cards.front: Memory types in a Cisco router and what each holds.
cards.back: RAM: running-config, IOS in execution, routing tables, ARP cache (volatile). Flash: IOS image (non-volatile). NVRAM: startup-config (non-volatile, small). ROM: bootstrap, ROMmon, mini-IOS — for boot-time and recovery (non-volatile).
